attack_surface.exposure: SignedData class owns signing and verification. All fields have internal set. Data.Signature is public set.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: External Data arriving via channels — Data.Signature could carry attacker-controlled SignedData
gaps: Data.Signature is { get; set; } — any code path can attach a SignedData object to any Data
ToSigningBytes() temporarily nulls Signature field (not thread-safe)
No algorithm whitelist — provider registry is the only gate

attack_surface.exposure: Nonce uniqueness enforced via in-memory MemoryStepCache.TryAddAsync
attack_surface.trust_boundary: External signed messages arriving at verify action
gaps: No nonce format validation (empty string accepted)

attack_surface.exposure: Private keys stored in System DataSource (SQLite), marked [Sensitive]
attack_surface.trust_boundary: SQLite file on disk, DataSource API
mitigations: [Sensitive] attribute filters private keys from JsonStreamSerializer output
Keys stored under System actor's DataSource (not User/Service)
gaps: SQLite is unencrypted — disk access = key access
[Sensitive] only filters serialization output, not dot navigation or raw JsonSerializer
ExportAsync returns raw private key string — by design (user-sovereign)

attack_surface.exposure: Assembly.LoadFrom loads arbitrary DLLs, discovers IProvider implementations via reflection
attack_surface.trust_boundary: .pr file parameters (path comes from builder output)
mitigations: Path resolved via FileSystem.Path.GetFullPath (relative to engine root)
gaps: No signature verification on loaded DLLs
Assembly.LoadFrom is full RCE — constructor can run arbitrary code
Path traversal mitigated by GetFullPath but no sandbox enforcement

attack_surface.exposure: Hash and verify actions with pluggable crypto providers
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Provider registry — only registered providers can hash
mitigations: DefaultCryptoProvider supports only keccak256 and sha256
Verify uses SequenceEqual (constant-time for equal-length byte arrays)
All methods return Data, never throw

attack_surface.exposure: Data.Envelope handles compression/decompression at transport boundary
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Inbound compressed payloads from external sources
gaps: Carry-forward: InvalidOperationException from RehydrateNestedData not caught (if Type parsing fails)

findings.vector: Data.Signature has public setter ({ get; set; }). External Data arriving via channels could have an attacker-crafted SignedData attached. However, VerifyAsync validates the cryptographic signature against the Identity (public key), so a forged SignedData without valid crypto will fail step 9.
findings.preconditions: Attacker controls Data object arriving at a verify action. Attacker must also have a valid signing key to forge signatures.
findings.impact: Without a valid private key, forged SignedData fails crypto verification. The public setter is a design smell but not exploitable without key compromise.
findings.exploit_sketch: Attacker sets Data.Signature to a crafted SignedData with modified contracts/headers but the same crypto signature → fails because ToSigningBytes() includes all fields in the signature, so any field change invalidates the signature.
findings.proposed_fix: Consider making Signature { get; internal set; } to limit write access to the signing pipeline. Not blocking because crypto verification is the real gate.

findings.vector: Nonce replay protection uses in-memory MemoryStepCache. In multi-process deployments, each process has independent cache — replay succeeds across processes.
findings.preconditions: Multiple PLang engine instances processing signed messages. Attacker captures a valid signed message and replays to a different instance.
findings.impact: Replay attack succeeds on a different instance. Single-process deployments are safe.
findings.exploit_sketch: Instance A verifies message (nonce cached). Attacker sends same message to Instance B (no cache entry) → passes all checks including nonce.
findings.proposed_fix: Document that distributed deployments require a shared cache implementation (Redis). ICache is already pluggable. Add a comment to SignedData.VerifyAsync noting this limitation.

findings.vector: provider.load uses Assembly.LoadFrom to load arbitrary DLLs. A .pr file specifying a malicious DLL path gets full RCE via constructor execution.
findings.preconditions: Attacker must control .pr file content (requires compromising the builder or manually placing a .pr file). The malicious DLL must be on the filesystem.
findings.impact: Full RCE. But this is user-sovereign: .pr files are trusted, and the user chose to load the DLL. Same trust level as any C# assembly load.
findings.exploit_sketch: PLang step: 'load provider ../../../malicious.dll' → builder writes to .pr → runtime calls Assembly.LoadFrom → RCE
findings.proposed_fix: No fix needed — this is by design. The user owns their .pr files. Document that provider.load is equivalent to Assembly.LoadFrom and carries full trust.

findings.vector: ToSigningBytes() temporarily nulls the Signature field, serializes, then restores it. Not thread-safe if SignedData is shared across threads.
findings.preconditions: SignedData instance accessed concurrently from multiple threads — one calling ToSigningBytes while another reads Signature.
findings.impact: Reader sees null Signature momentarily. PLang executes steps sequentially per context, so this is theoretical.
findings.exploit_sketch: Thread A: Signature=null → serialize → Signature=saved. Thread B reads Signature between null and restore → sees null.
findings.proposed_fix: Use a copy instead of mutation: serialize a temporary object with Signature=null rather than mutating this. Low priority — PLang is single-threaded per context.

findings.vector: Nonce sourced from MemoryStack GUID. No validation that nonce is non-empty or has minimum entropy. Empty nonce maps to cache key 'nonce:' — all empty-nonce messages share the same cache key.
findings.preconditions: MemoryStack GUID returns empty or very short string (unlikely but not validated).
findings.impact: First empty-nonce message passes, all subsequent fail (fail-secure). Attacker can't exploit this — it's self-denying.
findings.exploit_sketch: If GUID somehow returns empty: first sign+verify works, second verify returns NonceReplay error. Self-denying, not exploitable.
findings.proposed_fix: Add guard: if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(nonce)) return error. Defense-in-depth, not blocking.

findings.vector: [Sensitive] attribute on IdentityVariable.PrivateKey only filters JsonStreamSerializer output. Raw JsonSerializer (DataSource persistence), dot navigation (%MyIdentity.PrivateKey%), and Data.Envelope serialization are not filtered.
findings.preconditions: User-sovereign: the user owns their keys. Dot navigation access is by design.
findings.impact: Private keys readable via dot navigation and stored in plaintext SQLite. This is the intended model — keys belong to the user.
findings.exploit_sketch: PLang step: 'write out %MyIdentity.PrivateKey%' → outputs private key. This is the user's prerogative.
findings.proposed_fix: No fix — user-sovereign design. Document that [Sensitive] is serialization-output only, not an access control mechanism.

findings.vector: Header values compared via ToString(). Type confusion: int(1) matches string('1'). Attacker could substitute header value types if they control the verify action's Headers parameter.
findings.preconditions: Attacker controls the verify action's Headers dictionary (comes from .pr file parameters — trusted).
findings.impact: Minimal — both sides go through LLM builder, which produces consistent types. Cross-type matching is a loose comparison, not an exploit vector.
findings.exploit_sketch: Sign with Headers={count: 1 (int)}. Verify with Headers={count: '1' (string)}. ToString comparison matches. Not exploitable because attacker doesn't control both sides independently.
findings.proposed_fix: Consider JSON-based comparison instead of ToString. Low priority — not exploitable in practice.

findings.vector: Carry-forward from data-envelope-architecture: InvalidOperationException from RehydrateNestedData (if Type parsing fails in Decompress) not caught. Only JsonException and InvalidDataException are caught.
findings.preconditions: Malformed compressed payload at transport boundary where inner Data has an unparseable type field.
findings.impact: Unhandled exception escapes Decompress → caught by Step-level catch(Exception) safety net. Returns generic StepError instead of typed DecompressError.
findings.exploit_sketch: Send compressed Data with type field that triggers InvalidOperationException during rehydration → Decompress doesn't return Data.FromError, throws instead → Step catch handles it generically.
findings.proposed_fix: Add catch (InvalidOperationException ex) to Decompress pipeline alongside InvalidDataException.

summary: The signing module is well-designed with a solid 9-step verification pipeline. The cryptographic foundation (Ed25519 via NSec) is sound. Key findings: (1) Data.Signature public setter is a design smell but not exploitable without key compromise — crypto verification is the real gate. (2) Nonce replay protection is single-process only — documented limitation, ICache is pluggable. (3) Provider.load is full RCE by design (user-sovereign). (4) Carry-forward InvalidOperationException gap in Data.Envelope.cs Decompress. No critical or high severity findings. Overall security posture: GOOD for the intended user-sovereign threat model.
